title: Egg on Polyester — Enzyme-Safe Fiber, Full Arsenal Available
stainChemistry: Egg is a protein+fat combination stain. Egg white (albumen) is almost pure protein — primarily ovalbumin — that coagulates irreversibly when heated above 62°C/144°F (the same chemistry as cooking). Egg yolk adds phospholipids (lecithin), cholesterol, and fat-soluble pigments (carotenoids that cause the yellow color). On polyester, egg bonds through physical entrapment in the fiber's surface microstructure rather than chemical bonding — polyester is a synthetic (PET polymer) with no hydroxyl groups for hydrogen bonding. This makes egg on polyester easier to remove than egg on natural fibers, BUT polyester is oleophilic (oil-loving), so the yolk's fat component can absorb stubbornly into polyester's polymer structure.
whyThisWorks: Polyester is enzyme-safe — protease attacks only protein, not PET polymer. This means you can use full-strength enzyme treatment without any fiber risk. The enzyme digests both the albumen and the protein component of yolk. For the fat/oil component (yolk), polyester's oleophilic nature means you may need a surfactant or POG solvent step that wouldn't be necessary on cotton. Dan Eisen's Method 1 (enzyme detergent) handles most egg stains. The carotenoid yellow color from yolk responds to peroxide oxidation. Polyester tolerates all standard chemistry — broad pH range (4.0–11.0), heat up to 140°C, all solvents.

spottingProtocol.technique: Scrape with spoon edge or bone spatula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dan Eisen: 'If stain has built up (e.g., egg), carefully scrape off excess with the edge of a spoon.' Dried egg forms a hard, brittle film on polyester that can be scraped off easily. Remove as much solid material as possible before introducing liquid — this prevents spreading the protein into clean fiber.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back with water gun
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 100°F. Heat cooks egg protein irreversibly.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with cold water from the back. Fresh egg white (albumen) is highly water-soluble and flushes out easily with cold water. The yolk component is less water-soluble due to its fat content but cold water removes the water-soluble portion. Watch for white/yellow transfer to the towel beneath.

spottingProtocol.agent: Protease enzyme digester (Prochem Power Burst or equivalent)
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 15–30 minutes. Dan Eisen: 'Wait at least ½ hour.'
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply enzyme solution and tamp with brush. Polyester handles brush action and warm water without issue. The protease digests ovalbumin and yolk proteins into water-soluble peptide fragments. On polyester, you can use full-strength enzyme with extended dwell — zero fiber risk. This is one of the easiest fiber+enzyme combinations. Let it work for at least 15 minutes (professional) or 30 minutes (Dan Eisen's home protocol).

spottingProtocol.agent: Warm water flush + NSD surfactant for yolk fat
spottingProtocol.technique: Flush warm, apply NSD to address fat component
spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm to hot — safe on polyester, and now safe for the stain since protein has been digested
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with warm water to remove digested protein. Then apply NSD to address the yolk's fat component. Polyester is oleophilic — it holds onto oil and fat more tenaciously than cotton. The NSD surfactant emulsifies the lecithin and cholesterol. Tamp with brush. If fat persists, a small application of POG solvent (A.L. Wilson NovaBone or Street's Blue Lightning) will dissolve the remaining yolk fat. Flush with VDS after POG.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 6% (ONLY if yellow carotenoid color remains)
spottingProtocol.instruction: If a yellow stain remains after protein and fat removal, it's carotenoid pigment from the yolk. On polyester, peroxide with ammonia catalyst and steam-chase is safe and effective. Polyester has excellent oxygen bleach tolerance. The peroxide breaks the carotenoid's conjugated double-bond system, destroying the yellow color.

spottingProtocol.technique: Complete flush, machine dry safe on polyester
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final water flush. Polyester can be machine dried once the stain is confirmed removed — polyester is heat-resistant (up to 140°C). However, if ANY residual egg stain remains, air dry and repeat treatment. Heat-set egg protein on polyester is difficult to remove even with enzyme.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Scrape off excess with a spoon. Flush with cold water from the back. Most fresh egg (especially egg white) comes out with cold water alone. Soak in cold water + enzyme detergent (Tide Original) for 30 minutes. Wash in warm water. Inspect before drying.

DRIED/SET: Scrape off hard dried egg residue. Soak in cold water + Tide for at least 30 minutes (Dan Eisen's minimum). The enzymes need time to break down the coagulated protein. Wash in warm water. If a yellow shadow from yolk remains, apply a dab of Dawn dish soap directly to the yellow and wash again — Dawn's surfactant cuts through yolk fat better than laundry detergent.

COOKED/HEAT-SET EGG: If egg was heat-set (ironed, dried in hot dryer while stained), soak in cold water + Tide for 2–4 hours to allow extended enzyme contact. The longer the soak, the more protein the enzyme can digest. Wash warm. For remaining yellow, OxiClean paste applied directly for 1 hour.
PREVENTION: On polyester tablecloths and shirts, treat egg immediately. Polyester's oil-loving surface makes yolk fat increasingly difficult to remove as it absorbs over time.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water as the first step — heat coagulates egg protein just like cooking. Cold water first, always.
NEVER use the dryer until stain is confirmed gone — even polyester's heat resistance can't prevent the visual damage of thermally set egg protein.
NEVER alcohol on egg stains — Dan Eisen's rule: alcohol sets protein stains permanently.
NOTE: Polyester is oleophilic — yolk fat absorbs more stubbornly into polyester than into cotton. May need a surfactant or POG solvent step that wouldn't be needed on natural fibers.

professional.name: Prochem Power Burst
professional.use: Enzyme prespray — the primary treatment for egg protein on polyester
professional.note: Full strength, warm water, extended dwell (15–30 minutes). Polyester is completely enzyme-safe — no fiber risk. Protease digests ovalbumin and yolk protein.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG
professional.use: Yolk fat removal on oleophilic polyester
professional.note: If NSD alone doesn't remove yolk fat, POG dissolves the lecithin and cholesterol. Apply after enzyme step, flush with VDS. Polyester handles all solvents well.

professional.name: Street's Blue Lightning POG
professional.use: Quick counter-spotting of fresh egg yolk fat
professional.note: Fast-evaporating VDS for production spotting. Apply, blot, evaporates clean. Ideal for fresh yolk fat at garment intake.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.note: With ammonia catalyst. Steam-chase safe on polyester. Polyester has excellent peroxide tolerance.

consumer.name: Tide Original Liquid
consumer.use: Enzyme detergent soak — the best single product for egg at home
consumer.note: Cold to warm water soak, 30 minutes minimum. Protease in Tide digests egg protein. Safe on polyester.

consumer.name: Dawn Original Dish Soap
consumer.use: Yolk fat cutting — apply directly to yellow fat residue
consumer.note: Dawn's surfactant is specifically designed for food fat emulsification. Apply a dab directly to the yolk stain, work in with fingers, wash. Effective on polyester's oleophilic surface.

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.use: Extended soak for cooked/set egg + yellow carotenoid color
consumer.note: Dissolve per label. Soak 2–4 hours for set stains. Sodium percarbonate releases peroxide that oxidizes the carotenoid yellow. Safe on polyester.

escalation.when: Egg has been heat-set (cooked onto the fabric by dryer, iron, or hot water) and enzyme treatment doesn't clear it after extended soak, or yolk fat has fully absorbed into polyester and surfactant/POG treatment leaves an oily shadow.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is egg on polyester. I treated with cold water and enzyme detergent. [A protein residue / yellow yolk stain / oily shadow] remains. The egg [was / was not] exposed to heat before treatment. Polyester may need a POG solvent step for the yolk fat component.'
escalation.specialistType: Any dry cleaner should handle egg on polyester easily. This is a routine stain on a durable fiber. The dry cleaning machine will dissolve any remaining yolk fat in the solvent. If they can't handle this one, find a new cleaner.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Egg is a combination wet-side/dry-side stain. Pre-spot with enzyme digester (warm, 15 min dwell) for the protein, flush. The yolk fat component dissolves readily in PERC or HC during machine cleaning — polyester is the ideal dry cleaning fiber. Pre-spot oil stains on polyester for best results since polyester is oleophilic. PERC is excellent on polyester (Grade A compatibility). HC is also safe. Post-clean inspection: if egg protein was missed in pre-spotting and was heat-set during the cycle, you'll see a white/yellow protein residue. Post-spot with enzyme on the board, flush, re-clean.
